prompt.content:
Use file “cards_patient_14” to access the entire medical history / record for the patient. Please use the patient tab as the source of truth for all demographic information. Disregard patient identifiers (Name, Age, DOB, MRN) found within the text of the clinicalnotes tab as they have been shifted for de-identification purposes

-----

Prompt:  A 55 year old man presents with vague right upper abdominal pain. Based on the patient's clinical history, what is the recommended first-line pharmacologic treatment for his primary diagnosis? Answers should be in line with the most recent clinical guidelines as of January 12, 2026.

golden_responses.golden_text_response:
The patient's CT scan shows hepatic steatosis. Of note, the patient was on amiodarone in the past for a brief period of time between November 2023 and January 2024.  Amiodarone has been linked to hepatic steatosis. However on the prior CT scan from 8/5/2024, after the patient had been off amiodarone for 7 months, the liver does not show any abnormal findings.  The current CT scan from 1/10/2026 is the first mention of hepatic steatosis. This makes amiodarone an unlikely contributor to the patient's current findings. 

When compared to a prior CT scan from 8/2024, this is a new finding.  His laboratory results show elevation of the liver enzymes (AST = 202 U/L and ALT 148 U/L).  This patient has a history of multiple metabolic risk factors (diabetes, obesity, hypertension and dyslipidemia) and meets the criteria for metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH), including exclusion of other etiology (e.g. drug-induced). The histology report shows the results of a liver biopsy which confirms hepatic steatosis. There is also evidence of perisinusoidal fibrosis with portal fibrosis which correlates with stage 2 fibrosis. 

The patient has a primary diagnosis of MASH with stage 2 fibrosis. He is also diabetes and overweight (BMI = 27 kg/m²). Given his clinical history, the recommended first-line pharmacologic treatment for his primary diagnosis is a GLP-1 based therapy. Semaglutide is a GLP-1 receptor agonist, and recommended first-line treatment for patients with MASH with stage 2 fibrosis.
